content.headline: Uneven cuts mean uneven cooking — when some pieces are twice the size of others, the small ones are burnt before the large ones are done.
points: Always cut a flat side first on round vegetables — a rolling onion is how you lose a fingertip
A dice starts with even planks, then even strips, then even cubes — mess up the planks and everything after is chaos
Mincing uses the tip anchored to the board as a pivot — rock the heel up and down, never lift the whole blade
Julienne strips should bend slightly without snapping — if they snap, they're too thick and won't cook evenly
content.tell_me_more: The difference between restaurant food and home food is often traced back to knife work. A restaurant cook dicing onions for service has every cube the same size because that onion is going into a dish where everything finishes cooking at exactly the same moment. Uniform size is a courtesy to the other ingredients. When you're at home and things taste slightly off — a bit of raw here, a bit of mushy there — uneven cuts are frequently the culprit, not the recipe.

missions.title: Diced Onion
missions.description: Dice one medium onion into a small dice — roughly quarter-inch cubes. Take your time on the planks and strips before you do the final cuts.
missions.why_it_matters: Onion is the most common diced vegetable in cooking; getting this right translates to almost every savory dish you'll ever make.
missions.prompt: Show your cutting board with the diced onion spread out. When you look at the pile, most pieces should look roughly the same size — not a uniform field of perfect cubes, but no pieces that are dramatically larger or smaller than the majority.
reflection_choices: Pieces look mostly uniform — I could see the size consistency across the pile
Good but some larger chunks remained — my planks or strips weren't all even
Significant size variation — some pieces were two or three times bigger than others
Rough chop rather than a real dice — need to work on the plank-to-strip-to-cube sequence

missions.title: Where the Inconsistency Came From
missions.description: Think about at which stage of the dice your pieces became uneven — the planks, the strips, or the final cuts.
missions.why_it_matters: Identifying where your cuts go off-track helps you practice the specific step that needs work instead of repeating the same mistake.
missions.prompt: At which step did you notice your cuts becoming uneven — when you made the planks, when you made the strips, or during the final cuts? Or was it when the onion kept moving because you hadn't established a flat side? Describe what made the shape hard to control.
